fact: January 15 is exactly 2 weeks after New Year's Day - that's when most people's resolutions start falling apart!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is smack dab in the middle of winter's deepest freeze, when even polar bears think it's chilly.
The sun rises super late and sets embarrassingly early on January 15 in northern places - some kids go to school in the dark and come home in the dark too!
January 15 falls right in the heart of Capricorn season, when the sea-goat constellation rules the zodiac like a stubborn mountain climber.
Tree sap is basically frozen solid on this date in cold places, making trees look like they're taking the world's longest nap.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere spots, January 15 is peak summer - perfect for melting ice cream and sweating through your shirt!
The moon phase on January 15 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it shines extra bright against all that winter snow up north.
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are in their deepest sleep around January 15 - they're basically furry zombies until spring.
January 15 sits exactly 25 days after the winter solstice, which means daylight is finally starting to creep back minute by minute in the north.
In really cold places, your breath creates instant fog clouds on January 15 that look like you're a tiny dragon puffing smoke.
Penguins in Antarctica are dealing with their summer breeding season around January 15, which means lots of adorable fuzzy penguin babies!
The number 15 makes January 15 fall exactly in the middle of the month - it's like the calendar's belly button!
